title: Dictionaries — Basics
subtitle: Store and retrieve data by meaningful keys
learning_objectives: Create a dictionary with string keys and mixed-type values
Access values using square bracket notation
Add new key-value pairs and update existing ones
Understand KeyError and avoid it with .get()
Check for key existence with the in operator

sections.heading: Creating and Accessing Dictionaries
sections.content: A dictionary stores data as key-value pairs inside curly braces {}. Keys are usually strings (though any immutable type works) and must be unique. Values can be any type. Access a value with dict[key]. If the key does not exist, Python raises a KeyError.
sections.code_example:
# Creating a dictionary
person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "Paris"}

# Accessing values
print(person["name"])   # Alice
print(person["age"])    # 30

# KeyError if key doesn't exist
# print(person["email"])  # would raise KeyError

sections.heading: Adding, Updating, and Safe Access
sections.content: Assign to a new key to add a pair: dict["key"] = value. Assign to an existing key to update it — the old value is replaced. To avoid a KeyError when you are unsure whether a key exists, use dict.get(key, default). It returns the value if the key exists, or the default (which is None if you omit it).
sections.code_example:
person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30}

# Add a new key
person["email"] = "alice@example.com"
print(person["email"])      # alice@example.com

# Update an existing key
person["age"] = 31
print(person["age"])        # 31

# .get() with a default fallback
print(person.get("phone", "N/A"))  # N/A
print(person.get("name", "N/A"))   # Alice

sections.heading: Checking Key Existence
sections.content: Use the in operator to test whether a key is present in a dictionary before accessing it. This is a clean, readable way to guard against KeyError in branches of logic. Note that in checks keys, not values.
sections.code_output:
True
False
No email on file

exercises.title: Build a Profile
exercises.prompt: Create a dictionary called profile with keys "username", "level", and "score" set to "coder99", 5, and 1200 respectively. Print the value of "username".
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a dictionary called profile with keys "username", "level", and "score" set to "coder99", 5, and 1200

# Print the value of "username"

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors

exercises.title: Add and Update
exercises.prompt: Start with car = {"make": "Toyota", "year": 2018}. Add a key "color" with value "blue". Then update "year" to 2022. Print the final dictionary.
hints: car["color"] = "blue" adds a new key if it does not exist
car["year"] = 2022 replaces the existing value for 'year'

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors
The output should show the updated year 2022

exercises.title: Safe Lookup
exercises.prompt: Given config = {"debug": True, "timeout": 30}, use .get() to print the value of "timeout" and the value of "retries" with a default of 3.
hints: config.get("timeout", 0) returns 30 because the key exists
config.get("retries", 3) returns 3 because 'retries' is not in the dict

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors
The output should contain 30 for timeout
The output should contain 3 as the default for retries

quiz.question: What happens when you access a key that does not exist using dict["key"]?
options: It adds the key with value None
quiz.explanation: Accessing a missing key with square bracket notation raises a KeyError at runtime. Use .get() with a default value to avoid this.

quiz.question: Which line correctly adds a new key "score" with value 100 to an existing dictionary called player?
quiz.explanation: Dictionary key-value pairs are added or updated with simple assignment: dict[key] = value. Lists have .append() and .insert(), but dictionaries do not.

quiz.question: What does person.get("email", "N/A") return if "email" is not in the dictionary?
options: It adds the key "email" with value "N/A"
quiz.explanation: .get(key, default) safely looks up a key. If the key does not exist, it returns the default value you provide — here that is "N/A". It does NOT raise an error or modify the dictionary.

quiz.question: What does "name" in person check?
options: Whether the value "name" is stored somewhere in the dictionary
Whether "name" is a key in the dictionary
Whether the dictionary has at least one entry
quiz.explanation: The in operator on a dictionary checks keys, not values. "name" in person returns True if "name" is a key that exists in person.

quiz.question: Which of the following creates a valid Python dictionary?
quiz.explanation: Dictionaries use curly braces {} with key: value pairs separated by commas. Square brackets [] create lists, and parentheses () create tuples. Curly braces with no colons create a set.

completion_message: You now understand dictionary basics! Key-value storage is one of the most powerful patterns in programming. Next you will learn to iterate over and manipulate dictionaries.
